children.title: Definitions of Terms
children.content: As used in this Act:
Abatement refers to the reduction or elimination, in whole or in part, of duties and taxes where payment has not been made;

Actual or Constructive Exportation refers to the exportation of goods, including the goods to be processed applicable to goods which are being in free circulation, to a foreign customs territory and are intended to remain permanently outside it;
Admission refers to the act of bringing imported goods directly or through transit into a free zone;

Airway Bill (AWB) refers to a transport document for aircraft used by airlines and international freight forwarders which specifically identifies the consignor and consignee who has the right to claim delivery of the goods when they arrive at the port of destination. It is a contract of carriage that includes carrier conditions, such as limits of liability and claims procedures. In addition, it contains transport instructions to airlines and carriers, a description of the goods, and applicable transportation charges;

Appeal refers to the remedy by which a person who is aggrieved or adversely affected by any action, decision, order, or omission of the Bureau, seeks relief from the Bureau, the Secretary of Finance, or competent court, as the case may be;
Assessment refers to the process of determining the amount of duties and taxes and other charges due on imported and exported goods;

Authorized Economic Operator (AEO) refers to the importer, exporter, customs broker, forwarding agent, legitimate transport provider, and to other entity duly accredited by the Bureau based on the World Customs Organization (WCO) Framework of Standards to Secure and Facilitate Global Trade, the WCO Supply Chain Management Guidelines and the WCO Safe Framework of Standards to promote trade facilitation and to provide a seamless movement of goods across borders through increased international supply chain security with the use of risk management and modern technology;

Bill of Lading (B/L) refers to a transport document issued by a shipping line, carrier, and international freight forwarders or non-vessel operating common carrier for carriage of goods and which identifies the consignor and consignee who has the right to claim delivery of the goods at the port of destination. It is a contract of carriage that includes carrier conditions, such as limits of liability and claims procedures. In addition, it contains transport instructions to shipping lines and carriers, a description of the goods, and applicable transportation charges.
Bureau refers to the Bureau of Customs;

Carrier refers to the person actually transporting goods or in charge of or responsible for the operation of the means of transport such as airplane, shipping lines, freight forwarders, or common carriers, non-vessel operating common carriers and other international transport operators;

Clearance refers to the completion of customs and other government formalities necessary to allow goods to enter or for consumption, warehousing, transit, transshipment, or to be exported or placed under another customs procedure.
Commission refers to the Tariff Commission;

Conditional Importation refers to the customs procedure under the RKC as a special procedure wherein certain goods can be brought into a customs territory for a specific purpose that allows for relief from import duties and taxes, such goods must be imported for a specific purpose and must be intended for re-exportation within a specified period and without having undergone any substantial change except in normal depreciation;

Customs Broker refers to any person who is a bona fide holder of a valid Certificate of Registration issued by the Professional Regulation Commission and the Professional Regulatory Commission Board of Customs Broker which is issued under Section 9209, as amended, otherwise known as the "Customs Brokers Act of 2004";
Customs Officer refers to any customs administrative unit that is authorized and accredited to perform all or any of the functions enumerated under customs and tariff laws;

Customs Office refers to a person appointed, designated from a clerk, employee, or private person, who, not being clerk or officer, may be employed by the Bureau, or must be employed in the functions of the Bureau, or may also refer to any employee of the Bureau;

Customs Bond refers to a written undertaking given to the Bureau by any person required by laws, rules and regulations to guarantee the performance of any obligation of the said person arising under the customs and tariff laws;
Customs Territory refers to the territory wherein this Act, the regulations, and other tariff laws are enforceable.

Declaration refers to the act of the cargo examiner and the customs officer of the Bureau, as the case may be, of entering an import declaration or a portion thereof in a form or using other official and public procedures prescribed under laws, rules and regulations;
Declarant refers to a natural or juridical person who executes, either in his or her own behalf or in behalf of the importation or exportation of goods;
Drawback refers to a refund or remittance of duties and taxes paid on imported products which have been used for re-export or used in the production or manufacture of goods subsequently exported;
Electronic Data Interchange (EDI) refers to the electronic transfer of information from computer to computer in standard agreed formats for the purpose of processing customs data.
Exemption refers to an exemption from the payment of customs duties and taxes for imported articles, as provided for by law.
Export Declaration refers to a statement made by the exporter and the place of exportation of the goods;
Exporter refers to a natural or juridical person or any organization, whether a government or foreign government, enterprise, who is the responsible for the export of goods;
Filing means the electronic submission of a formalized document, including the submission of a duly signed export declaration or import declaration, is an electronic form if required by the Bureau;
Flagrant Violation refers to any of the following:
Failure to comply with the prohibition against importation of counterfeit goods under a trademark, patent, or copyright law and piracy under Republic Act No. 8293 or intellectual property rights;
Failure to comply with the importation of goods that are the subject of a patent, trademark or copyright litigation; or

When the volume of importation is so large, the dutiable value, taxes or duties and the charges due in respect thereof, exceed one hundred million pesos (P100,000,000.00) unless there is a mitigating or normalizing circumstance in the event the responsible person, natural or juridical, imported the goods in violation of this Act;
Foreign Exchange refers to the foreign currency in or through which proceeds or payments are made on goods imported and exported;

Free Zone refers to the special zones found in the country, designated and operated in accordance with the laws governing their operations, such as Republic Act No. 7934, 7935, and other applicable laws, except those portions in the Free Port;
Goods refers to articles, wares, merchandise and any other items which are subject of importation or exportation;
Goods Declaration refers to a statement made in the prescribed form by the declarant and other agencies affected for the importation of goods, exportation of goods, and their release from the Bureau;
Importation refers to the act of bringing goods of whatever kind from a foreign country into the Philippines, regardless of whether for consumption, warehousing, or admission in a free zone;

Freight Forwarder refers to any person or entity representing the forwarder in the operations of the international business of importation and exportation, or as agent of the principal of an importer, and any other related undertaking including activities provided in customs law and regulations.

Jurisdiction of the Customs Bureau refers to the collection and enforcement of customs duties and taxes, prohibitions and restrictions and control of customs authorities over all persons, goods, conveyances and other things under the customs authority, including customs ports, airports, seaports, piers, harbors, marinas, bridges, and other vessels or ports, whether or not any of these things are exclusively located in the territory of the Republic of the Philippines, and other waters of the Republic of the Philippines, whether or not any of these things are exclusively located in customs ports, whether or not any of these things are exclusively located in other Philippine territories, or in any other Philippine territory, or in the customs territory of the Republic of the Philippines.
Non-Vessel Operating Common Carrier (NVOCC) refers to a person, juridical or natural, or any vessel utilized by the same and any other related undertaking, provided in this Act; and

Outright Smuggling refers to the act of importing goods without first having presented the goods to the customs authorities, or without the goods having been declared, or having been declared in a different form or having falsely declared the same, which goods are seized.
Person means any individual, partnership, corporation, association, and the like;
Port of Entry refers to a domestic port open to both domestic and international trade, including principal ports of entry and subports of entry, through which goods may be carried and inspected; and
Port of Exit refers to a port or other place, including an airport, that constitutes a point of departure of goods from the customs territory.
Refund is when duties and taxes are refunded.
Reimportation means exportation of goods which have been imported;
Release of Goods refers to the action by the Bureau to permit goods undergoing clearance to be placed at the disposal of the party concerned;
Refund refers to the return, in whole or in part, of duties and taxes paid on goods;
Security refers to any form of guarantee, such as a surety bond, cash bond, standby letter of credit or irrevocable letter of credit, which ensures the satisfaction of an obligation to the Bureau;

Smuggling refers to the fraudulent act of importing any goods into the Philippines, or the act of assisting in concealing, buying, selling, disposing, or transporting such goods, knowing them to have been imported contrary to law, or the fraudulent exportation of goods. Goods referred to under this definition shall be known as smuggled goods;
Taxes refer to all taxes, fees and charges imposed under this Act and the National Internal Revenue Code (NIRC) of 1997, as amended, and collected by the Bureau.

Technical Smuggling refers to the act of importing goods into the country by means of undervaluation, misdeclaration or fraudulent misclassification, including incorrect quantity or weight, for the purpose of evading or avoiding payment of customs duties, taxes, fees and other charges;
Tentative Release refers to a case where the assessment is disputed and pending review, on the importer's and surety bond for the duties and taxes due on imported goods.
Transit refers to the customs procedure under which goods, in their original form, are transported under customs control from one customs office to another, or to a free zone;

Transshipment refers to the customs procedure under which goods are transferred under customs control from the importing means of transport to the exporting means of transport within the area of one customs office which is the office of both importation and exportation;

Traveler refers to any person who temporarily enters the territory of a country in which he or she does not normally reside and who will leave that country; and any person, who leaves the territory of a country in which he or she normally resides (departing resident) or who returns to that territory (returning resident); and
Third Party refers to any person who deals directly with the Bureau, for and on behalf of another person, relating to the importation, exportation, movement or storage of goods.

children.title: When Importation Begins and Deemed Terminated
children.content: Importation begins when the goods are brought or introduced into the Philippine territory with the intention to unlade them. Importation is deemed terminated when:

The duties, taxes and other charges due upon the goods have been paid and the goods, the duties, taxes and other charges for the goods are free from duties, taxes and other charges and local payment for such goods have been paid; or
In case the goods are deemed free of duties, taxes and other charges, the goods have legally left the jurisdiction of the Bureau.

children.title: When Duty and Tax are Due on Imported Goods
children.content: Except as otherwise provided for in this Act or in other special laws, duties and taxes are due upon the imported goods at the time of importation, including goods previously exported from the Philippines. Unpaid duties, taxes and other charges, shall incur legal interest of twenty percent (20%) per annum from the date of final assessment under Section 429 of this Act, when such payment becomes due and demandable. The legal interest shall likewise accrue on any fine or penalty imposed. Upon payment of the duties, taxes and other charges, the Bureau shall issue the necessary receipt or document as proof of such payment.

children.title: Effective Date of Rate of Import Duty
children.content: Imported goods shall be subject to the import duty rates under the applicable tariff heading that are effective as of the date of importation or upon withdrawal from the warehouse for consumption. In case of withdrawal from a free zone for introduction to the customs territory, the duty rate as of the time of withdrawal, or as of the time of introduction to the customs territory as the Bureau deems proper, which is the earlier time of withdrawal of the goods, the duty rate as of the time of introduction to the customs territory as the Bureau deems proper. In case of goods sold at customs public auction, the duty rate at the date of the sale under the provisions of implementing Section 114(e) of this Act shall apply.

children.title: Declarant
children.content:
A declarant may be a consignee or any person who has the right to dispose of the goods. The declarant shall lodge a goods declaration with the Bureau and may be:
(a) The importer, being the holder of the bill of lading; or
(b) The exporter, being the owner of the goods to be shipped out; or
(c) A customs broker acting under the authority of the importer or from a holder of the bill; or
(d) A person duly empowered to act as agent or attorney-in-fact for such holder.

In case the consignee or the person who has the right to dispose of the goods is not present in the customs territory, the importer's agent or employee may be authorized as declarant.

The goods declaration submitted to the Bureau shall be processed by the declarant or by a licensed customs broker. The Bureau shall issue implementing rules on this matter within sixty (60) days from the effective date of this Act; those passed by the provisions of this Act shall be forwarded to the Congressional Oversight Committee; Provided, That after two (2) years from the effective date of the Act, the Bureau shall implement the provisions of this section with international standards and customs best practices.

children.title: Rights and Obligations of the Declarant
children.content:
The declarant shall be responsible for the accuracy of the goods declaration and for the payment of all duties, taxes and other charges due on the imported goods. The licensed customs broker shall also be responsible for errors, except for the payment of duties, taxes and other charges due on the imported goods.

The declarant shall sign the goods declaration, even when acting as a licensed customs broker, who likewise signs the goods declaration.

children.title: Penalties for Errors in Goods Declaration
children.content: The Bureau shall not impose substantial penalties for errors when such errors are inadvertent, unintentional and are not fraudulent in nature or gross negligence in the commission thereof: Provided, That in case a declarant commits substantial penalty for errors without reasonable cause, a penalty may be imposed but not to be excessive.

children.title: Application of Information and Communications Technology
children.content:
In accordance with international standards, the Bureau shall utilize information and communications technology to enhance customs control and to promote, as required, the implementation of the provisions of the customs and customs procedures aimed towards a paperless customs environment.

The Bureau shall communicate, exchange and process trade- and logistics-related information in the national and regional level for the efficient and prompt clearance of goods and the establishment of a technology-neutral and secured infrastructure for businesses, industries, and government.

The security of data and communication shall be in a manner that is consistent with applicable internationally accepted standards on information security.

The Bureau shall likewise include as part of its systems and processes a disaster recovery and business continuity plan and implementing the objective of the Bureau's electronic and online services.

For purposes of customs procedures, electronic documents, permits, licenses or certificates shall be receivable and have the legal effect, validity or enforceability as any other document as legal writing: Provided, That when the prescribed procedures are duly complied with, the Bureau shall:
(a) Recognize the authenticity and reliability of electronic documents;
(b) Transmit approval in the form of electronic data messages or electronic documents; and
(c) Require and/or accept responses and issue receipts as well as acknowledgment of such electronic data messages or electronic documents.

The introduction and implementation of information and communications technology shall be undertaken with due consultation with affected parties and stakeholders.

children.title: Relationship Between the Bureau and Third Parties
children.content:
Parties may transact business with the Bureau either directly or through a designated third party to act on their behalf.

The customs transactions directly transacted by a party shall be subject to the same rights and obligations and the same requirements as those transacted through a designated third party.

A designated third party shall have the same rights and obligations as the designating party when transacting business with the Bureau.

Subject to the provisions of existing laws, treaties, conventions, and international agreements, the Secretary of Finance shall make the necessary regulations for the defined relationship between the Bureau and third parties.

children.title: Information on General Application
children.content:
All laws, decisions, rulings, circulars, memoranda and orders of the Bureau shall be published in accordance with law.

To foster an informed compliance regime, the Bureau shall ensure that all relevant and available information on general application pertaining to customs operations and procedures which are not confidential or intended for the Bureau's internal use only, shall be readily accessible to any interested person.

Any new information, amendment or changes in customs procedures or requirements shall be made available to the public, as practical as possible, to the extent that the information, amendment or changes in customs procedures or requirements apply:
A designated third party shall be published or otherwise made available by the Bureau's internal use only, relating to a specific matter as may be requested by an interested party for legitimate use.

children.title: Information of Specific Nature
children.content:
The Bureau shall provide interested parties, upon their written request, with information regarding customs matters that were requested.

The Bureau may require the payment of a reasonable fee for providing such information. The requested information shall be released within a reasonable time from the filing of the request and payment of the required fee.

children.title: Decision and Ruling
children.content:
The Bureau shall, consistent with Section 102(e), publish all decisions, rulings, decisions and rulings made by the Bureau.

Upon written request of the interested party, the Bureau shall notify the party of its decision in writing within the period prescribed by the Bureau upon receipt of the request or as prescribed in the implementing rules and regulations.

The Bureau shall, to the extent possible, publish its decision and rulings as prescribed by the Bureau in the implementing rules and regulations.

The ruling and decision shall be issued by the Bureau within thirty (30) days from the submission of the necessary documents and information.

children.title: Right of Appeal, Forum and Ground
children.content:
Any party adversely affected by a decision or omission of the Bureau pertaining to an importation, exportation, or any other lawful decision shall have the right to appeal within fifteen (15) days from receipt of the notice of appeal.

An appeal in writing shall be filed within the period prescribed in this Act or by regulation and shall specify the grounds therefor.

The Bureau may allow a reasonable time for the submission of supporting evidence to the appeal.
